title: 2026/03/17 ENV (Part 2)
description: Tuesday, 17 March 2026 | Environment Committee Planning Bill; Natural Environment Bill Air BNB Forest Owners Association Wellington Water Te Uru Kahika - Regional and Unitary Councils Aotearoa NZ Telecommunications Forum Woolworths New Zealand Taituara Watercare Port of Tauranga Ltd Insurance Council NZ
descriptionSummary: Planning Bill. Planning Bill; Natural Environment Bill. Air BNB

chunks.text: W- welcome back everyone to the Planning Bill and Natural Environment Bill, hearings this afternoon. I can see that we already have our next submitter with us, Airbnb. Uh, so we've got Catherine, we can see you there, and Louise. Uh, welcome to the committee this afternoon. You have 20 minutes with us, so, look, we'll hand it over to you to talk us through your submission and make some opening remarks, and then we'll open it up for questions. So over to you.

Wonderful. Thank you for the opportunity to address the committee today. Uh, my name's Catherine Cossevas, and I'm the acting head of public policy at Airbnb for Australia and New Zealand. I am joined today by Louise Espin, senior associate at Russell McVeagh. I will start by spending the first eight to 10 minutes sharing information about Airbnb and key points from our submission. Uh, my comments relate specifically to short-term accommodation and the opportunity presented by the planning bill to introduce a nationally consistent regulatory framework

for the sector. Short-term accommodation is a vital component of the New Zealand economy, supporting local jobs and small businesses throughout the country. Our community of hosts are everyday Kiwis who are passionate about hosting and showcasing all that their communities have to offer. They provide a range of accommodation options at a variety of price points. Short-term accommodation also plays an important role in the, in the visitor economy.

Every day, guests use our platform to travel within New Zealand, whether it be for a holiday, a work trip, to visit friends and family, or to attend a major event. We strongly support the government's broader objectives for planning reform, particularly in simplifying the planning system, improving consistency, and enabling economic growth.

And our key message today is, is quite simple. As New Zealand undertakes comprehensive planning reform, it would be a missed opportunity not to address greater national standardization and enablement of short-term accommodation regulation at the same time. In terms of our overall position, short-term accommodation is currently regulated in a highly fragmented way across New Zealand.

Different councils use different definitions of short-term accommodation. There are different consent requirements, different operating thresholds, and, and different zoning rules. And some examples of this include different definitions. So some plans only define one activity, for example, in Queenstown, that would be residential visitor accommodation, whereas others have two separate definitions, as in Christchurch, where both hosted visitor accommodation and unhosted visitor accommodation is defined. And another example is differing

rules by activity status. Short-term accommodation is a permitted activity with permitted standards in most, but not all residential type zones in key cities and towns. And with 78 councils that maintain separate district plans with varying rules, zones, and consent pathways for short-term accommodation, hosts and platforms like Airbnb must navigate a patchwork of rules, and this often creates regulatory uncertainty, unnecessary compliance costs, administrative burdens for councils and other organizations, as well as individuals that otherwise need to actively get involved in

long and expensive plan review, or plan change processes, and then potentially appeals also. Um, and so this can result in inconsistent outcomes, for communities. And, and to that end, naturals-- national standardization would be entirely consistent with one of the central goals of planning reform itself, which is greater national consistency. Short-term accommodation is, is fundamentally a land use issue. It concerns how residential property is used. The government has emphasized enabling people to use their private property as they wish, provided it does

not create adverse effects for neighbors. The basic activities taking place in, in short-term accommodation properties are sleeping, eating, bathing. Uh, this is the same as, as any residential use. In many jurisdictions internationally, including parts of Australia and the United States, short-term accommodation is treated as a form of residential use, for this reason. There is therefore no clear justification for treating short-term accommodation fundamentally differently from other residential occupancy.And as you're

aware, the planning bill already introduces nat- national instruments, including national standards, which would be the natural place to address this issue. National standards would allow short-term accommodation to be treated uniformly across the country, significantly reducing regional discrepancies, and our key submission is, is in support of the planning bill, which sets up the framework for national standards to be established for national consistency, for issues such as this.

And so while the content of national standards will be developed at subsequent stages, Airbnb's submission focuses on the processes for developing the standards being set up in the right way, including requiring key stakeholder engagements in the preparation of national instruments and allowing more than 20 days for submissions. We, we'd actually suggest 40 working days. And if, if short-term accommodation is not dealt with on a nationally consistent basis under this planning reform process, it would then require resource intensive processes under each land use plan

and result in further costs for councils, stakeholders, and also individuals. And so for efficiency and clarity, it makes sense to address short-term accommodation properly now, once, and within this reform process. Um, but I'll now touch on the economic and tourism benefits of, of short-term rental accommodation. Um, so the benefits are closely aligned with the planning bill's objective of supporting and enabling economic growth, and change through the development and use of land. Short-term rentals contribute significantly to New Zealand's

visitor economy, providing accommodation choice, flexible supply, options across different locations and different price points. Importantly, short-term accommodation spreads tourism spending beyond traditional accommodation hubs, including in smaller and remote areas of New Zealand that do not have many or any, hotel or motels.

In 2025, our host community welcomed over 3.3 million guests, to New Zealand. In 2024, Airbnb guests who stayed across the country spent an estimated $4.4 billion on accommodation and non-accommodation expenses, supporting more than 33,000 jobs, according to research by Oxford Economics.

And guests in short-term accommodation spend money in local cafes, in restaurants, shops, transport services and, and visitor attractions as well. And this, of course, directs tourism dollars into local communities, that may not have otherwise benefit from, from tourism. Hosting also supports Kiwi households. In a recent survey of Airbnb hosts in New Zealand, almost 40% said the income from hosting helps them to stay in their home, and this shows that short-term accommodation provides meaningful support for many households.

For, for so many Kiwis, hosting isn't merely a side activity, but it's a practical way to manage rising living costs and service mortgage repayments, particularly for first home buyers and also owner occupiers. And given these benefits, it, it is important that overly restrictive or inconsistent regulations don't unnecessarily limit responsible hosting.

Doing so could undermine a valuable source of income for Kiwi households and also reduce the flow of tourism revenue that supports local businesses and local communities. Um, and as I mentioned, the current fragmented system creates significant administrative burdens for local councils who are currently interpreting, enforcing, and updating complex and differing short-term accommodation rules.

The Queenstown District Plan review litigation, for example, lasted over six years and is a clear example of how fragmented regulation can lead to lengthy disputes, costly legal proceedings, uncertainty for, for all stakeholders, and substantial use of council resources. A clear and national framework that focuses on better enabling short-term accommodation and reducing the re- requirements for resource consents for certain activities would reduce the need for litigation, streamline compliance, and allow councils to focus their resources on more pressing planning priorities

such as enabling housing supply. Um, and so in closing, we strongly support the objectives of the planning bill. Establishing clear and consistent national rules for short-term rentals would directly support those objectives by reducing regulatory fragmentation, as I mentioned, improving efficiency in the planning system, supporting economic growth, and enabling responsible use of residential property. Um, and as I've mentioned, this reform process presents a unique opportunity to address New Zealand's approach to short-term

accommodation properly. Um, and we would welcome the opportunity to work constructively with government on the development and drafting of the national standards. Our goal is to work together to help get the framework right the first time. Uh, but thank you very much, for your time. Look, thank you very much, Catherine, for that, submission.

It's, interesting to hear from short-term rentals and accommodation, 'cause we haven't yet. So you're the, the first one we've heard from in this perspective, in this context. Uh, look, I'll ask a question, because there's only a couple of amendments that you've got in your submission that you're kind of asking for, and obviously one is to add a whole clause in at Clause 46 or a subsection. Um, and you've kind of outlined there, and it's to do with the national instruments.

D- Can you just outline how that's actually going to impact you or Airbnb and, and why that, that, that by adding that, it's impactful and significant for you? Yes. Thank you for the question. I will pass over to Louise Espin from Russell McVeagh to, to address this one on, on my behalf.

Hi there. Can everybody hear me? Yes, we can, Louise. Brilliant. Um, so the intention is, so that would be a change to Clause 46, which is the process for effectively making national instruments. So the change that Airbnb is seeking in the submission is to effectively enable a process where key stakeholders would be involved in the establishment of these national, standards, national instruments.

So that would be pre-submission stage. Mm. That would be in the sort of drafting of and the, the, the making of the instruments. Now, why that would be beneficial, and in this case, for Airbnb, in the case of a, a, a new national standard that is related to the short-term rental activity, is that Airbnb would be able to... And other providers such as Bookabach a- and, and other relevant, key stakeholders, would then have input into the drafting so that operational issues, sort of real-life, on-the-ground issues can be addressed at that kind of higher level prior to

it being drafted and then going to the submission stage. Acknowledging that there would still be a, a sort of standard submission process, but having that input up front could be ben- would be beneficial for kind of key organizations and reduce the need for that kind of t*t for tat down the track.

Yeah. Okay. And so just a supplementary on that. So key stakeholders, when you put that wording in there, that's obviously not just targeted at Airbnb, but you're feeling that n- more broadly, then there would be, more input. Yes, that's right. And I suppose for this clause it would have to be broad in order to apply to- Yes. Yeah... any, national standards. But yes, it would be relevant, relevant key stakeholders relevant to the national instrument in question.

Okay. And so just, and just quickly off the back of that, that, so if you gave a, a, a, as you say, an on-the-ground example, you kinda touched on a couple, Catherine. But, like, if you were giving, you know, an, an example where this perhaps could impact a lot of Airbnb, a- accommodation.

In terms of an example of how Airbnb's input could sort of help from- Yeah. Yeah, yeah, yeah... level? Like where... We... Yes. Yeah. Yeah. So it would probably be if, if it was a, let's say a national standard, so, like, national environmental standards we, we currently have, it would be input into the, you know, rule framework that would, ultimately then drop down to the, to the plans.

It would be input into the rule framework based on Airbnb's experience with relevant districts and, and, you know, district plan review processes around the country so that a sort of appropriate framework was created at that higher level. But, but had Airbnb been involved in a lot of, resource management, you know, planning in, in, you know, previously, like, from your o- your umbrella organization, not just obviously the, the people that have the houses?

Yes. Okay. Yeah. Yes. In- including I think Catherine made reference to the Queenstown context. So, Airbnb was involved from the, the earliest stage until resolving the appeal, six plus years later. Um, Airbnb's been involved with, with Christchurch and various other, other cities.

Yeah. Okay. Interesting. Thank you. Um, Grant's got a question. Yeah. Thank you very much. Um, I must admit that was a great sell job on Airbnb. I mean, it's... Um, and, and it's a, it's a great opportunity for people to, as a, you know, to make a few dollars or to an opportunity to rent out, to rent out their houses. I do think you, you didn't mention the fact that actually some of these people turn up and have big parties at these places.

So that, that sort of stuff would have to come in. When you, so when you talk about rules and so forth, I think it's more than just a quiet residential use of a property. So I suppose that's all the sort of things you wanna, rules and stuff that you wanna get consistent across the country when you, we, at, at why you wanna set standard set of rules, so you don't wind up with those sorts of, you, you know, bespoke rules in different parts of the country.

Makes it easier. Yeah. We, we have a, a global party ban policy. Um, so we just, we prohibit disruptive and unauthorized parties in Airbnb listings on our platform. Doesn't mean they don't happen. Um, there are consequences for those who attempt to, to violate these rules. Uh, we, ranging from account sus- suspension to removal from the platform. Um, and we are committed to promoting safe and responsible travel on Airbnb and in local communities. And this includes taking steps to help tackle those rare safety related incidents, like disruptive and

unauthorized parties, which aren't welcome on our platform by our hosts or in the, in local communities in which we operate. Um, and we continually invest in measures like technology and tools and resources for hosts, law enforcement and neighbors also, to help enforce our ban on disruptive parties and deter isolated issues. Um, the vast majority of our community are considerate neighbors and travelers, and we take action against those, who violate our strict policies.

Um, and in terms of, of the planning bill, we see, this opportunity for national consistency in the laws that apply, particularly from a planning sense, that would provide clear settings for, for hosts, guests, platforms, and also the broader community. Yeah. Thank you. Look, thank you very much for your submission.

It appears we don't have any further questions. But look, a really interesting perspective, and we appreciate the time you've spent on putting this submission together, and, and we'll take on board that feedback and those considerations, so thank you. Thank you very much. Thank you. So moving on to our next submitter for today, the Forest Owners Association

Can see you there, Elizabeth. Welcome- Kia ora... to the select committee this afternoon. Um, you are very familiar with this process, so, we will hand it over to you. Uh, obviously take your submission as read and, if you talk us through the main points, and then we'll open up for questions. Thank you.

Sounds great, thanks. Uh, kia ora koutou. I'm co-Elizabeth Hague, te hunga. So I'm the CEO of Forest Owners Association. Um, I'm gonna be joined today by Rachel Miller, who's our environment manager, and we've also got beaming in from Auckland, Jill Chapple and Trish Fordyce, who have been providing our environment committee with legal support through the, um...

in case she throws any curve b***s through, through the, the question time. So, so we'll just- we'll try to keep the presentation, we've, we've tried to shorten it down so that we can have plenty of time for discussion. Um, so look, Forest Owners Association, as you all are, are familiar with, represents the majority of New Zealand's production forestry estate, so that's, timber producers.

Um, so we've got approximately 1.2 million hectares by area that are our members, employing, across the forestry and wood supply chain just over 40,000 people, and we sequester about half of New Zealand's carbon emissions. Forestry operates as a long-term cyclical land use with distinctive and specific regulatory needs.

We rely on the health of the natural environment for our production, so these bills are very important to us. Because investment in forestry spans decades, forestry is highly sensitive to regulatory certainty, consistency, and proportionality. Regulatory settings must acknowledge the full production cycle, starting with land preparation through decades of growth and maintenance, through to harvesting and replanting.

Um, overall, we support the intent of the reforms and the funnel approach. Um, our concern is that some of the structural and design choices risk undermining and strea- the streamlining intent of the reform, particularly for long rotation land uses like forestry. We would like to as well highlight, we attached to our submission a report that we commissioned from NZ IER around the cost of compliance.

Um, look, I, I think the most important takeaway if, if you read nothing else in that report, is that the design choices we make here have a disproportionately large impact on small scale operators, and we know that the forest industry is, is made up of a lot of farm foresters. Um, and our submission was joint with the Farm Forestry Association.

So, so just wanna highlight that one of our kinda key concerns today as we're talking through this are those small grower operators. Um, the continuation of sector-specific national direction, is really important for the forestry sector, and so the, the ability of this legislation to enable that is critical to us.

Um, the national environmental standards for commercial forestry have delivered national consistency. They've embedded risk-based tools into regulation so that we can manage the risks associated with, forestry operations. Um, they've reduced the council by council reinterpretation by standardizing some of the definitions and the activities, and they've allowed regulatory effort to focus on genuinely high-risk activities.

Um, we, we really want to talk a little bit more about that because the bills need to be very careful about how they allow councils to depart from national settings, and to avoid refragmenting forestry regulation at a time when, we've got a lot of uncertainty, both globally and domestically.

Um, with that, I might hand over to Jill to comment a little further on some of those system design risks. Uh, kia ora Elizabeth, kia ora to the committee. Um, so in the forest industry submission, we highlight concerns about duplication across the planning bill and the natural environment bill, and really see this as a bit of a lost opportunity to streamline some of those issues that we're currently experiencing, with the RMA. So duplication for forestry is really an issue, particularly for harvesting and earthworks, essentially because these are simultaneously land

use activities and activities with environmental effects. So under the current system, we've got unitary councils, and these generally tend to operate more efficiently in this space. Uh, it avoids the need for dual consenting, increased costs and delays, a- and what we would really like to see is, is, this reconciliation of the overlap, between these two bills, ironed out here so that we have some more, consistency. So currently we see that the bills perpetuate the overlap of functions.

We're not clear about which act prevails to which part of an activity, which council is responsible, or how consent duration and conditions may well be determined. So I don't think that these, are fatal. Uh, they can be, uh... there are some solutions which we have attempted to identify in the submission.

Um, so for example, looking at, making it clear about national direction and how that can be used to clarify overlap and functional responsibility. Uh, and in particular, I think we would also highlight, that support for the retention of the NESCF as a tool to manage all of those activities associated with forestry and, and, and that in itself substantially assists with some of those overlap issues.

So I'm gonna pass over to Trish, to talk about activity status. We con- concerned about the loss of permitted and controlled activity status. Um, in effect, the proposed permitted activity process, it's, it's not a permitted activity. What you're creating is a new consent process. Refer you to Section 202 of the NEA, which requires assessment by the council of any lodged material.That's a consent process.

Section 38 of the PA and 39 of the NEA, with all those requirements for registration, written approval, certification, bonds, they're actually creating an unquantified, costly, and complex new process. Um, our report from the NZ- EIR actually is probably the only evidence out there as to the cost of a permitted activity process with th- the monitoring that is included in there. And if you read that, you'll see what those costs are actually there, or are there, and that would be across the whole of New Zealand, not just for any other rural land use, pastoral and

horticulture, but for all the urban area as well. Um, so this creating a- another consent process is kind of counterintuitive to the intent of the reforms. We're then concerned also with the loss of controlled activities. The NESCF and other NESs do use controlled activities, but sparingly. But they are there to provide certainty, so that people can operate subject to councils being able to assess specific, local conditions. And for n- for most of us, it's in forestry, it's the harvesting.

If you don't meet a permitted activity standard, you cascade to controlled. But you have the certainty that you will be, allowed, but subject to conditions. And I do refer you to other individual forestry companies which have supplied specific examples of how controlled activities are actually working for them, in New Zealand at this stage.

Elizabeth, indigenous biodiversity, I think. She's frozen. Oh, we're trying to pop back to Elizabeth, who- Oh... seems to have frozen there. It's okay. If we can't get her back, um- We can do it... we can talk to that. Okay. No, she's popped back. She's popped off there, so do you just want to perhaps talk to that?

Yeah, I think- There we go. You're back again, Elizabeth. Oh, here we go. So they were just talking to you. Yeah, sorry. Don't know what happened there. Our internet dropped out. Okay. You... And, so have you, you... A- are you now talking to another point there, or did you wanna open up for questions? Elizabeth?

Oh, look, I think, where... Did you guys, um- You're on it... have you covered off indigenous biodiversity at this stage? No. It's your turn. We've missed that, no. Okay. So just before we, before we go to questions, I guess the, the, important to cover off that the, the biodiversity value of plantation forest is often misunderstood. Forestry is highly supportive of the net gain approach to indigenous biodiversity and invests significantly in outcomes which generate net gain. Um, the ec- ecological health limits that are introduced is a new concept, and we just wanna get

some clarity around the, the workability of that. Um, so for instance, you know, as a former DOC employee, I know that there's a real lack of baseline data across the motu for biodiversity. So part of what we would like to understand is, how do we establish the ecological baselines? Um, how would significant natural areas be identified and, and mapped?

And our, our strong preference is for, spatial identification. And then thinking about, dev- developing that national consistency. So treating biodiversity as both a goal and a limit creates some uncertainty, and, we... we're also interested in understanding more about the regulatory relief provisions and how those are gonna be applied.

I guess there's some operational uncertainty around how that would all work, but with the proviso that we're doing a lot of work in this space around net gain. And so we're overall supportive, if we can get the settings to work. Um, with that, look, I'll just say, we can, we can cover off a little bit in the conversation around environmental limits, I think. Um, but, we really are looking for a system that's nationally consistent, risk-based, integrated across councils, and supportive of long-term investment.

With targeted amendments, we think the bills can strengthen environmental protection and enable sustainable forestry that supports regional economies and New Zealand's climate goals. So with that, happy to open for questions. Thank you, Elizabeth, and thank you all for your submission. Um, obviously a very important industry and, you know, we're mindful of these considerations, so thank you for the work you've put into this submission. Um, we'll go first to Ryan Hamilton, who's had his ques- hand up for a question.

Thanks, Chair. Thank, thanks, Elizabeth and team. I just wanted to take a bit of a, a, a macro view. You make some c- clear points throughout your submission, and obviously one of the first points you raise is about the, NES on commercial forestry and wanting to retain some of those existing provisions in terms of clarity for your role. Um, so I guess my question is, had, have you got that at the moment, given the, the flux of policy statements or the absence of some? Um, and my other point, you talk about it's pleasing to see the removal of the effects management

hierarchy, and that you're gonna get a much more proportionate, potentially, way to mitigate, challenges in that. So putting with, just some context on those two points, and then just generally as it sits at the moment, do you see this, the, the legislation, helping your industry?

Or is it, is it still, is it sort of more, more of a hindrance, putting aside, you know, your submissions on duplication and all those sorts of things? Yeah, so I might start at the back first and then, throw to Jill to talk a- talk a little bit more. Um, I think overall, like, uncertainty in terms of long-term investment is, is really challenging.

Um, so for forestry, until we're clear on how the bills handshake with each other and what the operating environment's gonna be, we're, we're kind of i- in the same place that we have been in. Um, and from an international perspective, when you look-- I've, I've had foresters from Australia say their experience when they had a n- a national planning bill and an environment bill is if the crosstalk's not, not m- managed effectively, then i- the consenting is still really, really difficult.

Yeah. Um, and, and I think the, the issue with that is you're not necessarily getting any better environmental performance for a lot m- more bureaucracy. So it's thinking about how do we get that certainty in place. Um, in, in terms of the design of, of the national direction, I, I think just to clarify our comments, one of the things we really like with the current national direction is that the, the risk cascade through an activity as you would undertake them in the forest. So where there are connected activities, those risks

have a belts and braces approach to management, for, for lack of a better analogy. So, so functionally, when we talk about the crossover with earthworks between the two bills, I guess the concern is, you know, w- we've seen that, that earthworks are something that's being impacted by climate change and in some regions is a higher risk activity.

Uh, having a lack of clarity about how that's gonna operate or how you are gonna manage that between the two bills is actually probably one of the riskiest areas for us, and that's why activities like that, we're really looking for some clarification about w- what the enabling overarching legislation's gonna be because at a regulatory level, we need th- those to be really clear and have a really clear risk-based approach to management.

Yeah. Um, Jill, is there anything else you- Thank you. And I'll just say that- So I was just gonna touch on your question, about the effects management hierarchy. Uh, we were pleased to see it removed from the bill because it had previously been in the NBAA, which is not to say that it doesn't have a place within the national direction, it's just that it seems to us that there is, a greater amount of flexibility to determine, where it appropriately sits within specific national direction rather than having it, you know, sitting right in the middle of the legislation

itself. Yeah. Yeah. So it's instead of making it more nuanced, instead of having it as a default- That's correct... regulation. Yeah. Thank you. Thank you. Look, we'll go to Lann Farm now. Sorry, my video keeps on popping off. There we go, Lann. E nga koutou, thanks f- so much for your submission.

Um, I wanted to bring up an example that h- that has caused some alarm with a number of submitters when it comes to regulatory relief, and this was, some comments that the minister for RM Reform made on radio where he said, in the example where, for example, up in, Tairāwhiti, the council might be seeking to put more stringent controls on forestry up there, that that council m- may be subject to paying forestry companies f- regulatory relief for basically putting those rules in

place. Now, I wanted to ask you about that, but I'm noting that you were more coming with questions about regulatory relief and how it might work. So I, I'd like to hear more from, you about how you think that could work in practice and sort of what the different issues or ideals would be from your point of view.

Elizabeth, do you want me to deal with that? Uh, yeah, you can start off, and then I, I think I'm happy to come in at the end. So yep, go ahead and have a first crack. Yeah, look, I think, the first thing with regulatory relief is, is we're not clear about when it's actually going to apply. Uh, and I think that, that one of the fundamental issues is that if there is already a control that arises in relation to land, just because there's new national direction, isn't going to change that situation which means that there's gonna be a lack of

eligibility for regulatory relief anyway. Our, our overall view is that, when we look at it, we see that there's actually very little application, for these provisions in a forestry context. Yeah. So just to add to that, I think when, when we talk, I think per- perhaps, s- subsequent to the minister's comments, some things have got conflated in the discussion around this. So, so first of all, like, that's, that discussion around regulatory relief in Tairāwhiti, I think what's got conflated there is we are having discussions about

concerns that we've got about the ETS liabilities for land use transition in Tairāwhiti. That, that's an immediate concern. That's not a concern with these bills. Is that where we, we are going to need to transition possibly 20 to 30% of some forestry areas, into, a different forest type, we've, we're gonna have to work with the government on what the ETS liabilities are there and how we might deal with some of those issues where, where ultimately that is gonna get transitioned into native forest.

That's a very different conversation to regulatory relief, and I think unfortunately those two things ended up getting conflated in, in some of the discussions in the national media. Yeah. Thank you. Um, look, I, I just had a further question around the permitted activity because it does come up quite a lot, in the submissions, and you've made a recommendation. Um, just in terms of as it stands at the moment, how do you see it, being-... playing out in practice.

You know, like in terms of the types of activity that we'd be seeing on the ground in the forestry industry. Well, one of the first things I think is important to say about permitted activities that I think is common- a commonly misheld perception is that permitted equals permissive. Mm.

So, so in order to undertake a permitted activity, you have to meet a range of criteria, and actually in, in a lot of cases our foresters will go ahead and seek consent where there's any uncertainty just to make sure that they're sweet with the council and that they're managing their risk effectively.

Um, the, the range of permitted activities that we've currently got in the NESCF kind of fall across the activity landscape. Um, I might throw to Jill and Trish on if there's any particular permitted activity examples they, they think would be useful to talk about in terms of how we operate in the forest.

The earthworks is a really, a good one in that the duplications district councils and regional councils was got rid of. In the NESCF, it's made really clear that we're permitted with regard to TAs, but with regard to, regional council functions, there's a cascade of the permitted activities through to, restricted discretionary.

And the permitted activities, what people forget in forestry, is that there is a mapping of erosion susceptibility. And so the risk and the level of concerns are different across those different, zones. So red zone ones are really tough and ri- erosion areas. Um, there are no very limited permitted activities for earthworks. But in yellow and green zones there is permission and but subject to you still have to supply, management plans, notification management plans, and you're still monitored and audited. So they've, they've set the thresholds at

different levels depending on the risk, the erosion risk, and the risk to the environment, and that's how that's working for, for earthworks. Okay. But I'm just, but well I suppose the question was that under the current bills, in terms of the permitted activity, where obviously the whole intent is to enable more permitted activity, but, um- Oh, yeah. Oh, yeah... I suppose, I suppose like it, it, yeah, I'm not quite understanding it from the forestry perspective. Right.

It wouldn't, it wouldn't happen, it wouldn't be enabled- Yeah... because for the regional council functions, which is land u- at the moment, and appears we don't quite know how the duplication's going to work out, but if you have, if earthworks are considered a regional council functions where there's an effect on the environment, in other words a discharge of sediment to land which could get to water, then the permitted activity is in effect a consent.

Because- Right... you, every bit of material that you put in is going to be assessed by the council. So first off, that whole permitted activity process for us, has actually been ratcheted up to, a resource consent process. Yeah. So that's one, one- That, that's what I suspected. I just, yeah, no, that makes sense. Thank you.

Okay. Um, Grant, time for one last question. Thank you, Madam Chair. Um, yeah, just a question.
